chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Computational Optimization. (3-0). Credit 3. Combinatorial theory of polytopes as a tool for the solution of combinatorial optimization problems; applications to max flow, matching and matroids; geometric interpretation of the results indicating the profound role that polyhedral combinatorics play in the design and complexity of approximation algorithms. Prerequisite: CSCE 629.

chunks.title: Course Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:

• Master basic theories and techniques in the current research in computational optimization.
• apply theories and techniques studied in the course to solve real-world problems in computational optimization.
• Use appropriate technologies to communicate, collaborate, and conduct research in the area of computational optimization.
• Gain a solid background and preparation for advanced research in computational optimization, and other related research areas.

chunks.title: Textbook and/or Resource Materials
chunks.content:
This material Is: Required
Introduction to Tractability and Approximability of Optimization Problems
Authors: Jianer Chen
Notes:
Will be posted in Canvas

This material Is: Recommended
The Design of Approximation Algorithms
ISBN: 978-0-521-19527-0
Authors: D. Williamson and D. Shmoys
Publisher: Cambridge University Press
Publication Date: 2011

chunks.title: Grading Policy
chunks.content:
• There are 3 homework assignments, used to test students' understanding of the course lectures. The final exam will be comprehensive. The course research project will provide students with opportunities to read recent published papers in the area and do research on problems currently interesting in the area.
• Homework: 10*3 = 30%, Course project: 35%, Final exam 35%.
• Grading Scale: A = 90-100%, В = 70-89%, C = 60-70%, F = 0-59%

chunks.title: Course Specific Late Work Policy
chunks.content: The assignments and project are due on the designated due dates at the beginning of class. No late submissions will be accepted. Discuss unusual circumstances in advance with the instructor.

chunks.title: Course Schedule
chunks.content:
• Week 1 (1/12-): Algorithmic background review
• Week 2 (1/19-): Maximum flow and graph matching
• Week 3 (1/26- ): Linear programming
• Week 4 (2/2- ): NP-completeness theory and approximation algorithms
• Week 5 (2/9- ): Approximation algorithms: FPTAS algorithms, homework #1 due
• Week 6 (2/16-): Approximation algorithms: PTAS algorithms
• Week 7 (2/23-): Approximation algorithms: PTAS algorithms (continued)
• Week 8 (3/2- ): Approximation algorithms: APX algorithms
• Week 9 (3/9- ): Spring break. No classes
• Week 10 (3/16-): Approximation algorithms: APX algorithms (continued), homework #2 due
• Week 11 (3/23-): Inapproximability results, PCP theorem
• Week 12 (3/30-): Parameterized algorithms
• Week 13 (4/6- ): Parameterized algorithms (continued), homework #3 due
• Week 14 (4/13-): ETH and Computational lower bounds
• Week 15 (4/20-): Exact algorithms for NP-hard problems
• Week 16 (4/27): Exact algorithms for NP-hard problems (continued), course project due
• May 1, Friday, Final exam (8:00 am - 10:00 am).

chunks.title: Additional Course Information
chunks.content: All course information will be posted in canvas. You should check your course announcements, course material and due dates daily. It is strongly recommended that you keep up with the course material and do not fall behind. Start working on assignments as soon as they are assigned. This course is using Canvas Learning Management System. Students submit their homework and projects via Canvas.

chunks.title: AI Statement
chunks.content: The use of AI is strictly prohibited in this course.

chunks.title: Course Specific Attendance Policy
chunks.content: Class attendance is strongly recommended. The student is responsible for any material missed. Missed exams may be made up only when the absence is university sanctioned. Medical reasons or other extenuating conditions beyond the control of the student must be properly documented. Discuss unusual circumstances in advance with the instructor when possible.

chunks.title: Texas A&M College Station
chunks.content: Students needing a listening ear can contact University Health Services at 979.458.4584. Call 911 or visit your nearest emergency room if you are currently experiencing a life-threatening situation or if your safety is at risk. 24-hour emergency help is also available through the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline (988) or at 988lifeline.org.
